content.text: Pagination and rate limiting are two foundational REST API design disciplines that together govern
how much data a client can retrieve in one request
how frequently it may make requests at all

. Without pagination, an endpoint that returns a list of resources must return every matching row at once — a single call against a million-row table would load all rows into memory, serialize them, and transmit potentially hundreds of megabytes to the client. Without rate limiting, a single misbehaving or malicious caller can monopolize server resources and degrade service for everyone.
is the mechanism by which a server splits a large result set into sequential pages and returns one page per request, along with a token or offset that tells the client how to request the next page.
is the mechanism by which a server enforces a quota on how many requests a caller may make in a given time window, returning
when the quota is exceeded, along with headers that tell the client when it may safely retry.

Both techniques appear in virtually every production REST API, from the GitHub API to Stripe's payment API to Anthropic's messages API. They are not afterthoughts added once a system becomes popular — they are architectural decisions that must be made during initial design, because retrofitting them onto an existing API forces breaking changes on clients that were written against the original unbounded interface.

The absence of pagination is one of the most common causes of production outages in data-backed APIs. A query that returns 20 rows in development returns 400,000 rows two years into production. The server runs out of memory serializing the response, the request times out, or the 200 MB response body overwhelms the client's JSON parser. These failures are difficult to diagnose because the endpoint worked perfectly in testing and the root cause — an unbounded
— is invisible until the data volume grows.

The absence of rate limiting enables a class of problems that range from accidental to malicious. A client with a bug that retries in a tight loop can generate thousands of requests per second. A competitor running a scraping bot can extract an entire product catalog. A distributed denial-of-service attack can exhaust connection pools. Rate limiting is the API's immune system: it enforces fairness between callers, protects backend services from overload, and gives API operators the levers to offer differentiated tiers of service.

For agentic AI systems, both problems are especially acute. An agent that loops over paginated results and makes a call per page needs a well-designed cursor API to do so efficiently. An agent that calls external tools rapidly — generating dozens of API requests per second on behalf of users — will hit rate limits unless it tracks quota consumption and paces itself. Understanding both techniques is therefore prerequisite knowledge for building reliable agentic pipelines.
The server materializes only one page of results at a time. A 50-row page uses the same server memory whether the underlying table has 100 rows or 100 million.
Bounded page sizes produce bounded response times. A 50-item page serializes in roughly the same time regardless of total table size.
Clients can request the page size that matches their use case: a mobile app may want 10 items per page, an export job may want 1,000.
Rate limiting ensures that heavy callers cannot crowd out light ones. Every client gets a guaranteed minimum throughput.
API operators can enforce per-tier quotas that align with pricing. Free-tier users get 100 requests/minute; pro-tier users get 1,000.
Rate limiting limits the damage a buggy client, a scraper, or a DDoS attack can inflict on shared infrastructure.
A paginated API lets a client process results as they arrive rather than buffering the entire result set, enabling streaming-style processing of large datasets.
Pagination adds client complexity. Instead of one request, the client must implement a loop that fetches pages until
is false or the cursor is null. Cursor tokens must be treated as opaque strings — a client that tries to parse or construct them will break when the server changes the encoding.
Cursor-based pagination supports only sequential traversal. Jumping to page 47 of 100 requires traversing all prior pages.
on a large filtered result set is often slower than fetching the first page. Avoid exposing total count by default.
A well-behaved client must parse rate-limit headers and implement backoff. Clients that do not handle 429 correctly will fail noisily.
Fixed-window rate limiters allow a burst of 2x the quota at window boundaries. Sliding-window algorithms eliminate this but require more storage.
If rows are deleted or reordered between pages, a cursor may skip or repeat items. Communicating this risk to clients is important.
The three dominant pagination styles differ in how they encode position in the result set:
in SQL. Simple to implement and allows jumping to any page, but becomes slow on large tables because the database must scan and discard the first 100 rows on every request.
token that encodes the position of the last item seen. The server decodes the token, runs a
query, and returns the next page. Stable and fast at any depth, but does not allow random access.
is cursor pagination applied to composite keys. A cursor over
. This works correctly with non-unique sort keys.
The four dominant rate-limiting algorithms differ in how they track request counts and handle bursts:
: divide time into fixed periods (e.g. each minute). Count requests per period. Reset at the boundary. Simple but allows 2x the quota at window edges.
: store a timestamp for each request in a sorted set; count how many fall within the last N seconds. Exact but memory-intensive.
: interpolate between two fixed-window counters to approximate the sliding window. Memory-efficient and accurate enough for most use cases.
tokens/second. Each request consumes one token. Allows controlled bursts up to bucket capacity. Used by Stripe, Anthropic, and AWS.

attrs.alt: Pagination and Rate Limiting architecture diagram

content.text: Each incoming request flows through three parallel checks before reaching the data store. The

inspects the caller's identity (API key or IP), looks up remaining quota in a fast store such as Redis, and either decrements the counter and passes the request through, or returns HTTP 429 immediately. The
decodes the opaque cursor token and translates it into a keyset predicate for the database query. The

component resolves the API key to a user and tier, which informs the rate limiter's quota lookup. The data store executes the bounded, indexed keyset query and returns rows. The server encodes the last row's key into a new cursor token and returns the envelope
Pagination should be the default design for any endpoint that returns a list. The question is not whether to paginate, but which style to choose:
for any feed, timeline, activity log, or event stream where results are naturally ordered by insertion time. This is the right choice in the overwhelming majority of production APIs.

only when clients genuinely need to jump to arbitrary pages (e.g. a search results UI where the user can click page 5 directly), and only when the table is small enough that OFFSET does not cause performance problems.
when your API needs to allow controlled short bursts (e.g. a client syncing after reconnecting) while still enforcing a sustainable average rate.
when strict fairness is required and bursts must be prevented absolutely (e.g. a high-value financial transaction API).
, not per IP address. IP-based limits fail for clients behind corporate NAT. Authenticated identity limits correctly attribute requests to the account consuming them.
Choose a page size default and maximum. A default of 20 to 50 and maximum of 200 to 1,000 is typical. Reject requests for more than the maximum with HTTP 400.
Design the cursor as a base64-encoded JSON blob containing the sort key(s) of the last item returned:
. Base64-encoding signals to clients that the value is opaque.
Build the keyset query using the cursor values. Fetch
rows: if you get that many back,
is true. Trim the extra row before returning the response.
Implement the rate limiter in a middleware layer, before business logic. Use Redis with the Lua-script pattern for atomic decrement-and-check in a single round-trip.
Return rate limit headers on every response, not just on 429s:
Index the database on the sort columns used for keyset pagination. Without an index on
, every page fetch is a full table scan.

# token_bucket.py -- pure-Python token bucket rate limiter (no external deps)

import time
import threading
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class TokenBucket:
    """
    Thread-safe token bucket.
    capacity : maximum tokens (= burst size)
    rate     : tokens added per second (= sustained rate)
    """
    capacity: float
    rate: float
    _tokens: float = field(init=False)
    _last_refill: float = field(init=False)
    _lock: threading.Lock = field(default_factory=threading.Lock, init=False)

    def __post_init__(self):
        self._tokens = self.capacity
        self._last_refill = time.monotonic()

    def _refill(self) -> None:
        now = time.monotonic()
        elapsed = now - self._last_refill
        self._tokens = min(self.capacity, self._tokens + elapsed * self.rate)
        self._last_refill = now

    def consume(self, tokens: float = 1.0) -> bool:
        """Return True if allowed; False if rate-limited."""
        with self._lock:
            self._refill()
            if self._tokens >= tokens:
                self._tokens -= tokens
                return True
            return False

    def retry_after(self) -> float:
        """Seconds until 1 token becomes available."""
        with self._lock:
            self._refill()
            deficit = 1.0 - self._tokens
            return max(0.0, deficit / self.rate)

    @property
    def remaining(self) -> int:
        with self._lock:
            self._refill()
            return int(self._tokens)


# Per-key bucket registry
_buckets: dict = {}
_registry_lock = threading.Lock()

def get_bucket(key: str, capacity: float = 60, rate: float = 1.0) -> TokenBucket:
    with _registry_lock:
        if key not in _buckets:
            _buckets[key] = TokenBucket(capacity=capacity, rate=rate)
        return _buckets[key]


if __name__ == "__main__":
    # 5-token burst, 1 token/second refill
    bucket = TokenBucket(capacity=5, rate=1.0)
    for i in range(1, 9):
        allowed = bucket.consume()
        status = "OK" if allowed else f"429 retry in {bucket.retry_after():.1f}s"
        print(f"Request {i:2d}: {status:<30} remaining={bucket.remaining}")
GitHub's REST API is a canonical example of both techniques applied well. The
, which a client follows until there is no
. Unauthenticated callers receive 60 requests per hour; authenticated callers receive 5,000. The current quota appears in every response:
Stripe's API uses cursor pagination based on object IDs.
returns the next 100 charges after ID
and the last item's ID. Stripe's rate limiter uses a token bucket with per-key and per-endpoint limits, returning
Anthropic's messages API enforces limits on requests per minute and tokens per minute per API key, returning

headers. An agentic system calling Claude in a loop must track these headers and insert delays when remaining capacity is low, to avoid task failures from unexpected 429 errors in the middle of a multi-step workflow.
Think of a REST API as a library's book delivery service, and think of the client as a patron requesting books.

Without pagination, the librarian must wheel every book that matches "science fiction" to the patron's desk in one trip — thousands of books, all at once, blocking the entire service for everyone else while the cart is being loaded.

is the rule that the librarian delivers books in batches of 20 and hands the patron a bookmark (the cursor) that marks exactly where they left off. The patron reads one batch, returns the bookmark, requests the next — working through the catalog at a sustainable pace.
Without rate limiting, any patron could monopolize every available staff member for hours.

is the rule that each patron may submit at most 60 delivery requests per hour. The token bucket model gives each patron a jar that holds 60 tokens (for bursting), refilled at 1 token per minute. Each request costs one token. If the jar is empty, the librarian hands back a note: "Come back in 42 seconds." Both rules together ensure that the library can serve many patrons simultaneously and that no single patron's large request degrades service for others.
SELECT * FROM events ORDER BY created_at LIMIT 50 OFFSET 10000

forces the database to read and discard 10,000 rows before returning 50. On a 10-million-row table, page 200 takes hundreds of times longer than page 1. Keyset pagination eliminates this: the database seeks directly to the cursor position using the composite index.
in every paginated response forces a
query on every page fetch. On a filtered query over a large table this is expensive. Omit total count from the default response and expose it only via a separate endpoint or an explicit

A corporation with 500 employees all behind a single egress IP address will be rate-limited as a single caller under an IP-based scheme, even though each employee is an independent user. Rate limit by authenticated identity (API key, user ID, or OAuth client ID) whenever authentication is available. Reserve IP-based limiting for unauthenticated endpoints.
header leaves clients guessing when to retry. They typically implement exponential backoff, which spreads retry load unevenly. Including
(seconds) allows well-behaved clients to retry exactly when the window resets.

A cursor that contains a plaintext SQL fragment or a numeric offset invites clients to construct cursors manually. When the server's storage backend changes, those hand-constructed cursors break silently. Always treat cursors as opaque base64-encoded tokens. Validate on decode and reject invalid cursors with HTTP 400.

A fixed-window limiter that allows 100 requests per minute can be gamed: a client sends 100 requests at 11:59:59 and 100 more at 12:00:00, delivering 200 requests in two seconds. Use a sliding-window counter or token bucket if burst prevention is important for your use case.
Choose cursor/keyset pagination for any new list endpoint. Offset pagination is acceptable only for small tables or when random page access is genuinely required.
Base64-encode the cursor payload. Document it as opaque in the API reference. Never change the encoding format without versioning the API.
Fetch one extra row to set has_more.
rows and trimming the last one is cheaper than a separate COUNT query.
Return rate-limit headers on every response.
to proactively throttle themselves before hitting the limit, reducing 429 errors.
Apply limits per authenticated identity, per endpoint.
A write-heavy endpoint may need tighter limits than a read endpoint. Separate quotas by HTTP method or endpoint class.
Cap page_size at a documented maximum.
Return HTTP 400 for requests above the maximum. Document the maximum in the API reference.
Version your API when changing pagination shapes.
Changing from offset to cursor pagination, or renaming
, is a breaking change. Use a
Store rate-limit counters in a shared store.
In-process counters break under horizontal scaling. Use Redis, Memcached, or a purpose-built rate-limit service that all application instances share.
Keyset pagination performance depends entirely on index design. The query
WHERE (created_at, id) > ($ts, $id) ORDER BY created_at, id LIMIT 50
runs in O(log n) time when a composite index on
exists. Without the index the database performs a full table scan on every page — O(n) — which becomes catastrophic at scale. Always run
on paginated queries in development and verify that the index is used.
If each item in a page requires an additional database lookup, use JOINs or batch lookups rather than one query per item.
A Redis-backed rate limiter adds one network round-trip per request. Co-locate Redis with application servers to keep this under 1 ms. A Lua-script atomic operation avoids a second round-trip.
Rate limiting and pagination both increase sustainable throughput when working correctly. Ensure database connection pools are sized for the expected concurrent page-fetch load.

The first page of a popular list can be cached at the CDN or in-process for a short TTL (5 to 30 seconds). Only cache pages without a cursor token, as subsequent pages depend on a cursor unique to each traversal.
The standard choice for distributed rate-limit counters. Supports atomic Lua scripts, sorted sets (sliding window log), and
Built-in IP-level rate limiting in nginx at the reverse-proxy layer, before requests reach application code.
API gateway products with configurable rate limiting, quota management, and pagination-aware response transformation without writing application code.
A rate-limiting library for FastAPI and Starlette, backed by Redis or an in-memory store. Decorates route handlers with
FastAPI has no built-in pagination, but the
library adds cursor and offset support. Use
relation types used by GitHub, GitLab, and many other APIs.
Useful for testing paginated clients: record real HTTP interactions (including 429 responses) once, then replay them in tests without hitting live APIs.
GraphQL's Relay Connections specification defines a cursor-pagination contract (
) that is conceptually identical to REST cursor pagination but expressed in a GraphQL schema. It is the right choice for GraphQL APIs. For REST APIs the simpler

Rate limiting is a security control as much as a reliability one. Without it, a single client can cause a denial-of-service by generating unbounded request volume, either accidentally (buggy retry loop) or deliberately. The rate limiter must be enforced before any business logic executes, in a layer that cannot be bypassed by application-level bugs.
Never rely on clients to self-throttle. A well-behaved client will, but a malicious or buggy one will not. The server is the enforcement point.
Decode and validate cursors before using them in SQL queries. A malformed or tampered cursor must return HTTP 400, not an unhandled exception or a SQL error that reveals query structure.
Cursor values extracted from tokens are user-supplied data. Always pass them as query parameters to the database driver, never by string interpolation into SQL.

If your API runs on multiple application servers without a shared rate-limit store, each server independently grants the full quota, effectively multiplying the allowed rate by the number of servers. Redis or another shared store is mandatory for correctness.
A sudden spike in 429 responses for a given API key signals either a buggy client or intentional abuse. Alert on this metric for fast incident response.
A global per-key quota can be exhausted by a lightweight list endpoint, blocking access to more important mutation endpoints. Per-endpoint or per-endpoint-class limits give finer protection.

Pagination is not optional — any endpoint that returns a list must paginate it. Default to cursor/keyset pagination for all new endpoints because it scales to any table size without performance degradation.
Cursor tokens must be opaque, base64-encoded, and validated on decode. Clients must treat them as black-box strings; the server controls the encoding and can change it with an API version bump.
rows and trim the last one to determine
cheaply, without a separate COUNT query.
Rate limiting must be enforced in shared infrastructure (Redis, API gateway) — not per-process — to correctly enforce quotas across multiple application server instances.
on every response, not only on 429s, so well-behaved clients can self-throttle before hitting the limit.

Agentic AI systems calling external APIs must parse rate-limit headers on every response and insert delays when remaining capacity is low, to avoid task failures from unexpected 429 errors mid-workflow.
Index the database on the sort columns used for keyset queries. Without a composite index, the paginated query degrades from O(log n) to O(n).

The token bucket algorithm is the industry standard for general-purpose rate limiting because it allows controlled bursts (useful for reconnecting clients) while enforcing a sustainable average rate over time.
